title: Brown soda bread
subtitle: Irish wholemeal quick bread with bicarbonate of soda
excerpt: A round, cracked loaf of brown soda bread leavened with bicarbonate of soda and buttermilk. Mixed in 5 minutes, baked in 35. The cross cut on top is not decoration: it allows the loaf to rise without splitting at the sides. Eat warm from the oven with butter.
sourceNotes: Soda bread is documented in Irish and British domestic cooking manuals from the early nineteenth century, the period in which bicarbonate of soda became widely available as a commercial raising agent. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the chapter on bread documents a quickly mixed wheaten loaf raised with bicarbonate of soda and sour milk, shaped on a baking tray and crossed with a deep cut before baking. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the bread chapter includes an Irish soda loaf with wholemeal flour and buttermilk, noting that the cross cut allows the dough to open and bake through without a long fermentation.

content.text: Soda bread is the most direct of all breads: mix the dry ingredients, add the buttermilk, shape, and bake. No proving time, no knead, no fuss. The leavening comes from the reaction between the bicarbonate of soda and the acid in the buttermilk, which happens in the oven. Speed matters: once the buttermilk hits the bicarbonate, the reaction starts. Get it into the oven within 5 minutes of mixing.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine the {{wholemeal-bread-flour}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl and stir to mix. Pour in the {{buttermilk}} and stir quickly with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together. It should be soft, slightly sticky, and shaggy, do not overwork it or the crumb will toughen.

Turn the dough out onto the lined tray and pat into a round about 20 cm across and 5 cm tall. Scatter the {{rolled-oats}} over the top if using. Using a sharp knife, cut a deep cross in the top (about 2 cm deep) that reaches almost to the edges of the loaf. This is the
. Get it into the oven within 5 minutes of mixing.

Bake for 35 to 38 minutes until the crust is deep brown and the loaf sounds hollow when you tap the base. The internal temperature should read 93°C. Cool on a rack for at least 10 minutes before cutting, the crumb is set but needs a moment to stabilise. Eat within the day; toast the following day.
